content.text: HTML tables represent two-dimensional tabular data — rows and columns where the meaning of each cell depends on its row header, column header, or both. Used correctly, a table communicates structure to both sighted users and screen readers. Used incorrectly — as a layout mechanism — it creates inaccessible, unmaintainable markup. Understanding the full element set, the
attribute, and Angular's data-binding patterns will cover every real-world table use case.

By default, HTML tables have separated borders and no consistent spacing. The most important CSS resets for a modern table:
is almost always required — without it, adjacent cells render a double border line.
overrides the browser default of centred header text, which is usually not what you want in a data table.

Wide tables overflow their container on narrow screens. The simplest fix wraps the table in a horizontally scrollable container. For very data-dense tables, consider a card layout at small breakpoints using CSS Grid or Flexbox.
control flow is ideal for rendering data tables. Define the column keys as an array to keep both the header row and the data rows in sync, and always provide a
expression to enable efficient DOM reuse.
renders a single spanning cell when the rows array is empty, providing a user-visible fallback without a separate
binding on the empty cell dynamically matches the number of columns so the row always spans the full table width.
Sorting is a common requirement. Store the current sort key and direction as component state, then re-sort the rows array in a computed signal or via an explicit sort method.

Unit tests for a data table component should verify that rows render from the data source, that the correct number of columns appears, and that the empty state is shown when the data array is empty.

Only a restricted set of CSS properties apply to

Rendering thousands of rows into the DOM hurts performance and usability. Client-side pagination divides the dataset into pages and renders only the current page. For extremely large lists, virtual scrolling (CDK) renders only the visible rows.
are signals and a computed signal. Angular's change detection updates only the table body when the page changes — no manual subscriptions needed. The pagination navigation exposes
so screen readers can find and describe the control.
When not to use a table

Tables are semantically correct only when both rows and columns carry independent meaning. If you only need to display a list of items in multiple visual columns, use CSS Grid or CSS columns — not a table. If the data is a single series (one value per item), use a definition list or a simple unordered list.
Rows and columns both have independent semantic meaning.

Visual columns only; no headers that span both axes.
Semantic relationship is label-for, not table-grid.
Using tables for layout. Layout tables break keyboard navigation and confuse screen readers. HTML tables are for data, not visual arrangement.

Mismatched cell counts with colspan/rowspan. If a rowspan absorbs a cell slot in subsequent rows, those rows must not include the absorbed cell — the browser silently shifts all later cells right, causing layout corruption.
